sections.name: Search & Sort
sections.summary: Foundational algorithms — binary search and bubble sort with step-by-step visualization.

sections.name: Data Structures
sections.summary: Core data structures — stack (LIFO) and queue (FIFO) with push/pop/enqueue/dequeue operations.

sections.name: Graph Algorithms
sections.summary: Graph traversal and shortest path — BFS and Dijkstra's algorithm.

1.title: Binary Search Fundamentals
1.focus: Understand divide-and-conquer search on sorted arrays
concepts: Binary search only works on sorted arrays
Start with low=0 and high=length-1, calculate mid = floor((low+high)/2)
If arr[mid] === target, found it. If arr[mid] < target, search right half (low = mid+1). Otherwise search left half (high = mid-1)
Loop continues while low <= high. If loop ends, target is not in array
Each step eliminates half the remaining elements — that's why it's O(log n)
Compare to linear search O(n): for 1 million elements, binary search needs ~20 steps vs 1 million
commonMistakes: Forgetting the array must be sorted first
Off-by-one errors: using low < high instead of low <= high
Calculating mid as (low+high)/2 which can overflow — safer: low + (high-low)/2
Updating low = mid or high = mid instead of mid+1/mid-1 (causes infinite loop)
realWorldScenarios: Looking up a word in a dictionary — you open to the middle, not page 1
Finding a specific commit in git log using git bisect
Database index lookups use binary search trees internally
1.successCriteria: Student can trace binary search step-by-step and predict which half gets eliminated

2.title: Bubble Sort Visual Run
2.focus: Understand comparison-based sorting through repeated passes
concepts: Bubble sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they're in the wrong order
Each pass 'bubbles' the largest unsorted element to its correct position at the end
After pass 1, the largest element is in place. After pass 2, the second largest, etc.
Optimization: if no swaps occur during a pass, the array is already sorted — stop early
Time complexity is O(n^2) — inefficient for large arrays but great for learning sorting concepts
It's a stable sort — equal elements maintain their relative order
commonMistakes: Forgetting that the inner loop shrinks each pass (already-sorted elements at the end)
Not implementing the early termination optimization (swapped flag)
Confusing bubble sort direction — largest bubbles to the END, not the beginning
Thinking bubble sort is efficient — it's O(n^2), only good for small/nearly-sorted arrays
realWorldScenarios: Sorting a hand of playing cards by repeatedly comparing adjacent cards
Understanding why O(n^2) algorithms don't scale — 10x data = 100x time
Recognizing when a nearly-sorted array makes bubble sort acceptable
referenceCode: for (let j = 0; j < arr.length - 1 - i; j++) {
2.successCriteria: Student can trace each pass, predict swaps, and explain the early termination optimization

3.title: BFS Graph Traversal
3.focus: Explore graphs level-by-level using a queue
concepts: BFS explores all neighbors of a node before moving to the next level
Uses a queue (FIFO) — enqueue the start node, then dequeue and enqueue its unvisited neighbors
A visited set prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs
BFS finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs (fewest edges)
The order of exploration depends on the order neighbors are listed
Time complexity O(V+E) — visits every vertex and edge once
commonMistakes: Forgetting the visited set — causes infinite loops in cyclic graphs
Adding a node to visited when dequeuing instead of when enqueuing (causes duplicates in queue)
Confusing BFS (queue) with DFS (stack/recursion)
Not understanding that BFS gives shortest path only in unweighted graphs
realWorldScenarios: Finding the shortest route between two subway stations
Social network — finding all friends within 2 degrees of separation
Web crawlers explore links level-by-level from a starting page
3.successCriteria: Student can trace BFS traversal order and predict the queue state at each step

4.title: Stack: Push & Pop
4.focus: Understand LIFO data structure with push and pop operations
concepts: A stack follows LIFO — Last In, First Out
push(item) adds to the top, pop() removes from the top
peek/top views the top element without removing it
Think of a stack of plates — you can only add/remove from the top
The call stack in programming uses this exact structure
Common operations are all O(1) — constant time
commonMistakes: Popping from an empty stack (stack underflow)
Confusing LIFO (stack) with FIFO (queue)
Not checking if stack is empty before popping
Using a stack when a queue is needed (order matters!)
realWorldScenarios: Browser back button — each page is pushed, back button pops
Undo/redo functionality in text editors
Function call stack — nested function calls push/pop frames
Matching parentheses in code — push open, pop on close
4.successCriteria: Student can trace push/pop operations and predict stack state after a sequence of operations

5.title: Queue: Enqueue & Dequeue
5.focus: Understand FIFO data structure with enqueue and dequeue operations
concepts: A queue follows FIFO — First In, First Out
enqueue(item) adds to the rear, dequeue() removes from the front
front views the first element without removing it
Think of a line at a store — first person in line gets served first
BFS uses a queue to explore level-by-level
enqueue and dequeue are O(1) with a linked list implementation
commonMistakes: Dequeuing from an empty queue (queue underflow)
Confusing FIFO (queue) with LIFO (stack)
Using array shift() for dequeue — it's O(n), not O(1)
Not understanding that queue order is the opposite of stack order
realWorldScenarios: Print queue — documents print in the order they were sent
Task scheduling in operating systems — processes wait in a queue
Message queues in web apps (RabbitMQ, SQS) — process jobs in order
Customer service — first caller gets answered first
5.successCriteria: Student can trace enqueue/dequeue operations and predict queue state at each step

6.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path
6.focus: Find shortest paths in weighted graphs using a priority queue
concepts: Dijkstra finds the shortest path from a source to all other nodes in a weighted graph
Uses a priority queue (min-heap) to always process the closest unvisited node first
Relaxation: if going through node U to node V is shorter than the current best path to V, update it
Initialize source distance to 0, all others to infinity
Greedy approach — once a node is finalized, its shortest path won't change
Only works with non-negative edge weights
commonMistakes: Using Dijkstra with negative edge weights (use Bellman-Ford instead)
Forgetting to initialize all distances to infinity except the source
Not using a priority queue (using a simple array makes it O(V^2) instead of O((V+E) log V))
Confusing Dijkstra (weighted) with BFS (unweighted shortest path)
realWorldScenarios: GPS navigation — finding the fastest route between two locations
Network routing — packets take the shortest path through routers
Flight booking — finding the cheapest route with connections
referenceCode: for (const node in graph) dist[node] = Infinity
6.successCriteria: Student can trace Dijkstra step-by-step, predict relaxation updates, and explain why negative weights break it
